FT_IC1.evidence: US ABCD Study, N=9,066 children baseline and 1-year follow-up (9-10 years old at baseline)

FT_IC2.evidence: Qualifies: social screen time (texting, social media, video chatting) + nonsocial screen time (YouTube, video games) both measured with separate coefficients. Applies B1 (YSTS with separate β for SM, texting, video chat)

FT_IC3.evidence: Regression models (cumulative logistic regression) with psychiatric symptoms as predictor of screen time AND screen time as predictor of psychiatric symptoms. ORs with 95% CIs reported (e.g., OR=1.22, 95% CI 1.22-1.23)

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple regression coefficients reported: weekday social OR=1.02 (95% CI 1.01-1.02), weekday nonsocial OR=1.22 (95% CI 1.22-1.23), weekend social OR=1.15 (95% CI 1.14-1.15), weekend nonsocial OR=1.10 (95% CI 1.09-1.10); rule breaking predicting weekday nonsocial OR=1.65 (95% CI 1.63-1.67)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD empirical analysis, longitudinal design with residualized change models

FT_IC6.evidence: Published in Social Psychiatry and Psychiatric Epidemiology 2024, no evidence of duplicate/preprint

FT_EC7_reason: FAILS condition (a): Paper has BOTH directions - psychiatric symptoms → screen time AND screen time → psychiatric symptoms. Models assess psychiatric symptoms as predictors of screen time AND screen time as predictors of psychiatric symptoms. Applies B18: bidirectional analysis disqualifies FT-EC7.
notes: Clear bidirectional analysis; psychiatric symptoms (not demographic-only) are predictors of screen time.

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD dataset used to examine causal relationships between screen use and brain development

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen use is measured as exposure variable; reading vs screen time compared. Qualifies as digital media/screen time variable (smartphone/device use)

FT_IC3.evidence: Causal analysis described in abstract; examination of causal relationships with coefficient reporting

FT_IC4.evidence: Abstract indicates results with numerical findings on brain development effects; full paper contains regression coefficients

FT_IC6.evidence: Published 2024, Advanced Science journal, no evidence of duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: FAILS multiple conditions. (a) Screen use is IV (predictor), not DV - paper examines causal effects OF screen use ON brain development/language ability, not demographics predicting screen use. (b) Outcome variables are brain development, language ability, behaviors - NOT demographic variables predicting SM.
notes: Screen use is independent variable predicting neurodevelopmental outcomes; not a demographic→SM longitudinal analysis.

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study data from three consecutive follow-up years (years 2, 3, 4), n=6,143 adolescents (mean age 12 years at year-2)

FT_IC2.evidence: Primary outcome is Video Game Addiction Questionnaire (VGAQ) scores, which measures problematic gaming symptoms. No qualifying smartphone or social media variable as independent/predictor variable. Gaming addiction symptoms are outcome, not independent SM/smartphone exposure variable. Applies B5: unspecified gaming (addiction symptoms, not device/platform use) NOT_MET.

FT_IC3.evidence: Bayesian hierarchical linear models examining longitudinal association between reward processing and VGAQ scores with β coefficient reported (β = -0.87, 95% CI: -1.68, -0.07)

FT_IC4.evidence: Bayesian regression coefficient reported: β = -0.87 (95% CI: -1.68, -0.07) for lower caudate activation predicting decreased VGAQ scores

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD empirical analysis with fMRI neuroimaging data and longitudinal design

FT_IC6.evidence: Published 2024 in Journal of Behavioral Addictions, no evidence of duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: Does not qualify because FT-IC2 is NOT_MET (no qualifying SM/smartphone variable). Additionally, gaming addiction (VGAQ) is outcome variable, not SM as dependent variable being predicted by demographics.
notes: Paper focuses on reward processing (fMRI activation) → gaming addiction, not SM/smartphone exposure as independent variable.
